title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: response y numeric predictor x categorical, statistical hypothesis test comparing central tendencies selected. check whether assumptions general linear model (see Appendix ) fulfilled, linear model lm(y ~ x) first fitted. Normality testing residuals: Shapiro–Wilk test (Shapiro Wilk 1965) (shapiro.test()) performed internally studentised residuals computed rstandard(), scales raw residuals estimate standard deviation accounts leverage observation (Cook Weisberg 1982). Shapiro–Wilk (SW) test powerful detecting non-normality across distributions, especially smaller sample sizes (Razali Wah 2011; Ghasemi Zahediasl 2012). Non parametric-tests: SW test rejects normality (pSW≤αp_{SW} \le \alpha), non-parametric tests selected: Wilcoxon rank-sum test (wilcox.test()) two groups, Kruskal–Wallis test (kruskal.test()) followed Holm adjusted pairwise Wilcoxon tests (pairwise.wilcox.test()) two groups. Parametric tests: insufficient evidence normality (pSW>αp_{SW} > \alpha) sample sizes large (50 observations per group), parametric tests selected. large samples central limit theorem ensures approximate normality sampling distribution mean. (Rasch, Kubinger, Moder 2011; Lumley et al. 2002; Kwak Kim 2017) homogeneity rejected (pL>αp_L > \alpha): two groups, Student’s t-test (t.test() var.equal = TRUE) applied; two groups, Fisher’s one-way ANOVA (aov()) Tukey’s Honestly Significant Differences (HSD) (TukeyHSD()) (Hochberg Tamhane 1987) applied. homogeneity rejected (pL≤αp_L \le \alpha): two groups, Welch’s t-test (t.test() var.equal = FALSE) applied; two groups, Welch’s heteroscedastic one-way ANOVA (oneway.test()) Games-Howell post-hoc test (games.howell()) (Games Howell 1976) applied. Welch’s methods outperform classical counterparts variances differ (Moser Stevens 1992; Fagerland Sandvik 2009; Delacre, Lakens, Leys 2017). Regardless sample size, assumption diagnostics always displayed. Throughout vignette, linear model refers classical Gaussian linear model framework (Searle 1971) underlying t-tests, ANOVA, linear regression. normality residuals met homoscedasticity rejected, visstat() selects Welch methods additionally displays group-wise normality diagnostics via vis_group_normality(), since Welch’s variance estimates group-specific pooled lm() residuals longer appropriate diagnostic heteroscedasticity. diagnostic plots enable users visually assess whether assumptions met manually override automated p-value-based test selection. decision logic based entirely p-values distributional assumptions; assess practical relevance. Users always report interpret appropriate effect size measure (e.g. Cohen’s dd t-tests, ω2\omega^2 ANOVA, rank-biserial rr Wilcoxon Kruskal–Wallis tests) alongside test result.

Staying within visstat(), correlation = TRUE gives assumption-free result cost causality:  preserve causality, follow second path fit Gamma generalised linear model log link. Gamma family suited Ozone strictly positive continuous, variance grows mean — structure detected Breusch–Pagan test. log link (canonical inverse link) preferred practice guarantees positive fitted values.  well-fitting Gamma generalised linear model, standardised deviance residuals asymptotically standard normal; use Shapiro–Wilk Anderson–Darling approximate checks.
code: result_ozone0 <- visstat(airquality$Wind, airquality$Ozone) ## Warning: Statistical assumptions violated: ## Normality of residuals violated (Shapiro-Wilk p = 0.00522 ) ## Homoscedasticity violated (Breusch-Pagan p = 0.00595 ) ## Analysis proceeded but interpret results cautiously. ## RECOMMENDATION: Consider exploring alternatives outside visstat() such as data transformations, generalised linear models, or robust regression. For an assumption-free, non-causal alternative consider rerunning with correlation = TRUE. result_ozone1 <- visstat(airquality$Wind, airquality$Ozone, correlation = TRUE) # Remove zeros to satisfy Gamma requirements airquality_clean <- subset(airquality, Ozone > 0) # Gamma model with log mapping model_gamma <- glm(Ozone ~ Wind, data = airquality_clean, family = Gamma(link = "log")) summary(model_gamma) ##  ## Call: ## glm(formula = Ozone ~ Wind, family = Gamma(link = "log"), data = airquality_clean) ##  ## Coefficients: ##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     ## (Intercept)   4.8155     0.1730  27.830  < 2e-16 *** ## Wind         -0.1196     0.0165  -7.247 5.42e-11 *** ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## (Dispersion parameter for Gamma family taken to be 0.4002672) ##  ##     Null deviance: 74.757  on 115  degrees of freedom ## Residual deviance: 50.432  on 114  degrees of freedom ## AIC: 1040 ##  ## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 8 # Extract standardised  residuals std_dev_res <- rstandard(model_gamma, type = "deviance") # Validate using the Shapiro-Wilk normality test shapiro.test(std_dev_res) ##  ##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test ##  ## data:  std_dev_res ## W = 0.99245, p-value = 0.7817 # Validate using the Anderson-Darling normality test nortest::ad.test(std_dev_res) ##  ##  Anderson-Darling normality test ##  ## data:  std_dev_res ## A = 0.198, p-value = 0.8853

text: construct hypothetical survey 150 secondary-school students alcohol consumption frequency academic performance recorded five-point ordinal scale. negative monotone association expected: students consume alcohol frequently tend achieve lower academic performance.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 150 # Latent scores with deliberate negative monotone association: # higher alcohol consumption (xs) -> lower academic performance (ys) xs <- sample(1:5, n, replace = TRUE) ys <- pmin(5, pmax(1, (6 - xs) + sample(-1:1, n, replace = TRUE))) likert_levels  <- c("never", "rarely", "sometimes", "often", "always") likert_levels2 <- c("poor", "fair", "ok", "good", "great") alcohol     <- ordered(likert_levels[xs],  levels = likert_levels) performance <- ordered(likert_levels2[ys], levels = likert_levels2) kendall_result <- visstat(performance, alcohol, correlation = TRUE)

what: Test decisions based solely on p-values of statistical tests
title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: single test maintains optimal Type error rates statistical power across distributions (Olejnik Algina 1987), p-values obtained tests may unreliable assumptions violated. Assessing assumptions solely p-values can lead type errors (false positives) type II errors (false negatives). large samples, even minor, random deviations null hypothesis can result statistically significant p-values, leading type errors. Conversely, small samples, substantial violations assumption may reach statistical significance, resulting type II errors (Kozak Piepho 2018). Moreover, assumption tests provide information nature deviations expected distribution (Shatz 2024). Thus assessment normality homoscedasticity never rely solely p-values complemented visual inspection diagnostic plots generated visstat().

what: Testing normality with the Shapiro–Wilk normality test
title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Normality tests behave poorly ends sample-size range: small samples fail detect non-normality, large samples flag negligible departures normality significant (Ghasemi Zahediasl 2012; Fagerland 2012; Franc 2025). package uses n>50n > 50 per group rule thumb threshold omit normality testing, assuming parametric tests central tendencies become robust non-normality larger sample sizes based central limit theorem. threshold necessarily arbitrary; simulation studies show convergence rate depends skewness kurtosis underlying distribution, moderately skewed distributions requiring roughly 40–50 observations adequate convergence sampling distribution mean (Fagerland 2012). Simulation studies suggest Shapiro–Wilk highest power among normality tests small moderate (n=10n = 10 100) sample sizes (Razali Wah 2011). package uses therefore Shapiro-Wilk test select parametric (ANOVA/Welch’s t-test) non-parametric (Kruskal-Wallis/Wilcoxon) methods groups smaller 50.

what: Appendix A: The general linear model
title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: general linear model (Searle 1971) provides unified mathematical framework underlying Student’s t-test, Fisher’s ANOVA, simple linear regression. Let nn denote number observations k−1k-1 number predictors. model observation ,=1,…,ni,\;= 1, \ldots, n : Yi=β0+β1xi1+⋯+βk−1xik−1+εi,εi∼N(0,σ2)Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \cdots + \beta_{k-1} x_{ik-1} + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2) YiY_i response observation ii, xijx_{ij} value predictor jj observation ii, β0,β1,…,βk−1\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_{k-1} kk parameters, εi\varepsilon_i independently distributed error terms constant variance σ2\sigma^2.

previous_headings: Appendix A: The general linear model
what: Student’s t-test as a linear model
title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Student’s t-test tests null hypothesis means two (unpaired) groups equal. create one indicator binary variable xi1x_{i1}, xi1=0x_{i1} = 0 observations group 1 xi1=1x_{i1} = 1 observations group 2. Taking expectations group: Group 1 (xi1=0x_{i1} = 0): E[Yi|xi1=0]=β0=μ1E[Y_i | x_{i1} = 0] = \beta_0 = \mu_1 Group 2 (xi1=1x_{i1} = 1): E[Yi|xi1=1]=β0+β1=μ2E[Y_i | x_{i1} = 1] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 = \mu_2 Therefore, β1=μ2−μ1\beta_1 = \mu_2 - \mu_1 represents difference group means. Testing H0:β1=0H_0: \beta_1 = 0 mathematically equivalent testing H0:μ1=μ2H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 Student’s t-test.

Even equal variances (s12=⋯=sk2s_1^2 = \cdots = s_k^2) equal sample sizes (n1=⋯=nkn_1 = \cdots = n_k), Welch test statistic algebraically identical classical ANOVA FF-statistic. Nevertheless, conditions Welch statistic converges classical FF-statistic, numerical differences become negligible practice (Welch 1951). t-tests special cases ANOVA. t-tests special cases ANOVA comparison two groups. squared tt-statistic equals corresponding FF-statistic: t2=Ft^2 = F. comparison two groups, Student’s t-test, t.test(var.equal = TRUE) aov() yield identical p-values, Welch’s t-test t.test(var.equal = FALSE) oneway.test(). case visstat() reports t-statistic, provides sign information (indicating group larger mean).

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Shapiro–Wilk test (Shapiro Wilk 1965) evaluates whether sample x1,…,xnx_1,\ldots,x_n comes normal distribution. Let x(1)≤⋯≤x(n)x_{(1)}\le \cdots \le x_{(n)} order statistics. Introduce reference sample Y1,…,YnY_1,\ldots,Y_n independent standard normal random variables, .e. Yi∼N(0,1)Y_i \sim N(0,1) ii, let Y(1)≤⋯≤Y(n)Y_{(1)}\le \cdots \le Y_{(n)} order statistics used construct Shapiro–Wilk weights. Define mi=E(Y()),=1,…,n,m_i = E\!\left(Y_{()}\right), \quad =1,\ldots,n, vij=Cov⁡(Y(),Y(j)),,j=1,…,n,v_{ij} = \operatorname{Cov}\!\left(Y_{()},Y_{(j)}\right), \quad ,j=1,\ldots,n, 𝐦=(m1,…,mn)⊤,𝐕=(vij),j=1n.\mathbf{m}=(m_1,\ldots,m_n)^\top,\qquad \mathbf{V}=(v_{ij})_{,j=1}^n. Thus, 𝐦\mathbf{m} contains expected positions standard-normal order statistics, 𝐕\mathbf{V} describes covariance structure. Let 𝐚=(a1,…,)⊤\mathbf{}=(a_1,\ldots,a_n)^\top resulting vector normalised weights ordered observed sample values 𝐚=𝐕−1𝐦(𝐦⊤𝐕−1𝐕−1𝐦).\mathbf{} =\frac{\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{m}} {\sqrt{\left(\mathbf{m}^\top \mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{m}\right)}}. Shapiro–Wilk statistic W=(∑=1naix())2∑=1n(xi−x‾)2.W=\frac{\left(\sum_{=1}^{n} a_i x_{()}\right)^2} {\sum_{=1}^{n} (x_i-\bar{x})^2}. WW takes values (0,1](0, 1]; values close 1 indicate normality. Simulation studies show Shapiro–Wilk test highest power among normality tests small moderate sample sizes (n=10n = 10 100100) (Razali Wah 2011). drives parametric/non-parametric branching decision visstat(); groups 50 observations normality assumed central limit theorem (Lumley et al. 2002; Rasch, Kubinger, Moder 2011). implementation uses shapiro.test() (R Core Team 2026).

text: Levene–Brown–Forsythe test improves upon Levene’s original test (Levene 1960) using median instead mean centre data. makes robust skewed data data outliers providing reliable results many practical situations (Allingham Rayner 2012). levene.test() mimics default behaviour leveneTest() car package (Fox Weisberg 2019). Levene–Brown–Forsythe test evaluates null hypothesis groups equal variances testing whether absolute deviations group medians equal across groups. observation yijy_{ij} group ii, computes absolute deviation group median: zij=|yij−yĩ|,z_{ij} = |y_{ij} - \tilde{y_i}|, yĩ\tilde{y_i} median group ii. test statistic F-statistic one-way ANOVA zijz_{ij} values: F=∑=1kni(z‾−z‾)2k−1∑=1k∑j=1ni(zij−z‾)2N−k=(N−k)∑=1kni(z‾−z‾)2(k−1)∑=1k∑j=1ni(zij−z‾)2F = \frac{\frac{\sum_{=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{z}_i - \bar{z})^2}{k-1}}{\frac{\sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (z_{ij} - \bar{z}_i)^2}{N-k}} = \frac{(N-k) \sum_{=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{z}_i - \bar{z})^2}{(k-1) \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (z_{ij} - \bar{z}_i)^2} kk number groups, NN total sample size, nin_i sample size group ii, z‾\bar{z}_i mean absolute deviations median group ii, z‾\bar{z} overall mean absolute deviations. null hypothesis equal variances, test statistic follows F-distribution: F∼F(k−1,N−k)F \sim F(k-1, N-k).

text: function visstat() accepts input three ways: standardised form, x y must vectors class "numeric", "integer", "factor". formula interface, formula y ~ x specifies response y predictor x variables, data data frame containing variables. backward-compatible form, "namex" "namey" must character strings naming columns data.frame named dataframe. column must class "numeric", "integer", "factor". equivalent writing: simplify notation, throughout remainder, data class numeric integer referred common mode numeric, data class factor referred categorical. interpretation x y depends classes: one numeric factor, numeric must passed response y factor predictor x. supports tests central tendencies. numeric, simple linear regression model fitted y response x predictor. factors (ordered), test association performed (Chi-squared Fisher’s exact). test symmetric, plot layout depends variable supplied x. factors y additionally class ordered, non parametric test performed: Wilcoxon test case two factor levels x, Kruskal-Wallis-Test two factor levels x. x y class ordered, order levels carries information Chi-squared / Fisher discard. visstat() instead tests monotone association via Kendall’s τb\tau_b (cor.test(..., method = "kendall")) visualises relationship jittered rank–rank scatter plus mosaic plot.

text: object class "htest" components: statistic value chi-squared test statistic. parameter degrees freedom. p.value p-value test. method character string indicating test performed. data.name character string giving name model.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
text: Breusch-Pagan test regresses squared standardized residuals fitted values. test statistic : $$BP = n \cdot R^2$$ : \(n\) = sample size \(R^2\) = coefficient determination auxiliary regression         \(e_i^2\) \(\hat{y}_i\) null hypothesis homoscedasticity, test statistic follows chi-squared distribution: \(BP \sim \chi^2(p-1)\) \(p\) number parameters model (including intercept). Large values test statistic (small p-values) provide evidence homoscedasticity.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
text: Breusch, T. S., Pagan, . R. (1979). simple test heteroscedasticity random coefficient variation. Econometrica, 47(5), 1287-1294. DOI: 10.2307/1911963

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
code: # Example with homoscedastic errors set.seed(123) x <- runif(100) y <- 2 + 3*x + rnorm(100, sd = 1) model1 <- lm(y ~ x) bp.test(model1)  # Should not reject (p > 0.05) #>  #> 	Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity #>  #> data:  model1 #> BP = 0.025212, df = 1, p-value = 0.8738 #>   # Example with heteroscedastic errors (variance increases with x) set.seed(456) x <- runif(100) y <- 2 + 3 *x + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5 + 2*x) model2 <- lm(y ~ x) bp.test(model2)  # Should reject (p < 0.05) #>  #> 	Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity #>  #> data:  model2 #> BP = 20.435, df = 1, p-value = 6.169e-06 #>

text: Performs Levene's test using  Brown-Forsythe modification (median-centred).  tests null hypothesis groups equal variances testing whether absolute deviations  group  medians equal across groups  function reproduces default behaviour  leveneTest(y,g,center=median,...) car-package.

title: Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) — levene.test
text: object class "htest" components: statistic value F-statistic. parameter degrees freedom: df1=k-1, df3=N-k, k number groups N total sample size p.value p-value test. method character string indicating test performed. data.name character string giving name(s) data.

title: Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) — levene.test
text: observation \(y_{ij}\) group \(\), compute absolute deviation group median: $$z_{ij} = |y_{ij} - \tilde{y}_i|$$ \(\tilde{y}_i\) median group \(\). test statistic F-statistic one-way ANOVA \(z_{ij}\) values: $$F = \frac{(N-k) \sum_{=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{z}_i - \bar{z})^2}{(k-1) \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (z_{ij} - \bar{z}_i)^2}$$ : \(k\) = number groups \(N\) = total sample size \(n_i\) = sample size group \(\) \(\bar{z}_i\) = mean absolute deviations group \(\) \(\bar{z}\) = overall mean absolute deviations null hypothesis equal variances, test statistic follows F-distribution: \(F \sim F(k-1, N-k)\).

title: Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) — levene.test
text: Brown, M. B., Forsythe, . B. (1974). Robust tests equality variances. Journal American Statistical Association, 69(346), 364–367. DOI: 10.1080/01621459.1974.10482955

title: Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test for Homogeneity of Variance (center = median) — levene.test
code: set.seed(123) y <- c(rnorm(10), rnorm(10, sd = 2), rnorm(10, sd = 0.5)) g <- factor(rep(1:3, each = 10)) levene.test(y, g) #>  #> 	Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test (center = median) #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group medians (for ANOVA on spread differences) #> F = 3.3913, df1 = 2, df2 = 27, p-value = 0.04853 #>   # Usage with data frame df <- data.frame(response = y, group = g) levene.test(response, group, data = df) #>  #> 	Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test (center = median) #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group medians (for ANOVA on spread differences) #> F = 3.3913, df1 = 2, df2 = 27, p-value = 0.04853 #>   # Example with unequal variances (should reject null hypothesis) set.seed(456) y_unequal <- c(rnorm(15, sd = 1), rnorm(15, sd = 5), rnorm(15, sd = 0.2)) g_unequal <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 15)) levene.test(y_unequal, g_unequal) #>  #> 	Levene-Brown-Forsythe Test (center = median) #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group medians (for ANOVA on spread differences) #> F = 31.384, df1 = 2, df2 = 42, p-value = 4.607e-09 #>

title: ANOVA or Welch's ANOVA with appropriate post-hoc tests — vis_anova
text: function first tests homogeneity variance using Levene's test. variances equal (p > alpha), Fisher's one-way ANOVA Tukey's HSD post-hoc performed. variances unequal (p <= alpha), Welch's one-way ANOVA Games-Howell post-hoc performed. function produces box plot jittered points group means (red diamonds parametric branches), annotated compact letter display showing groups differ significantly.

title: ANOVA or Welch's ANOVA with appropriate post-hoc tests — vis_anova
code: # Example with equal variances (uses Fisher's ANOVA + TukeyHSD) data(PlantGrowth) result1 <- vis_anova(PlantGrowth$weight, PlantGrowth$group,                       samplename = "Weight", factorname = "Group")   # Example with unequal variances (uses Welch's ANOVA + Games-Howell) # Create data with heterogeneous variances set.seed(123) group_a <- rnorm(20, mean = 10, sd = 1) group_b <- rnorm(20, mean = 15, sd = 5)  # Much larger variance group_c <- rnorm(20, mean = 12, sd = 2) values <- c(group_a, group_b, group_c) groups <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 20)) result2 <- vis_anova(values, groups,                       samplename = "Value", factorname = "Group")

title: Wrapper for visstat_core Allowing Three Different Input Styles — visstat
text: wrapper supports three input formats: (1) Formula interface: visstat(y ~ x, data = df), formula specifies response (y) predictor (x) variables, data data frame containing variables. (2) Standardised form: visstat(x, y), x y vectors class "numeric", "integer", "factor". x predictor grouping variable y response variable. (3) Backward-compatible form: visstat(dataframe, "name_of_y", "name_of_x"), first character string refers response variable second predictor grouping variable. must column names dataframe. interpretation x y depends variable classes. Throughout, data class numeric integer referred numeric, data class factor referred categorical: one variable numeric factor, numeric vector response (y) factor grouping variable (x). supports tests central tendencies (e.g., t-test, Welch's ANOVA, Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis). variables numeric, linear model fitted y response x predictor. variables factors, association test (Chi-squared Fisher's exact) used. test result invariant variable order, visualisations (e.g., axis layout, bar orientation) depend roles x y. wrapper standardises input calls visstat_core, selects executes appropriate test visual output assumption diagnostics.

title: Wrapper for visstat_core Allowing Three Different Input Styles — visstat
code: # Formula interface mtcars$am <- as.factor(mtcars$am) visstat(mpg ~ am, data = mtcars)     # Standardised usage visstat(mtcars$am, mtcars$mpg)     # Backward-compatible usage (same result as above) visstat(mtcars, "mpg", "am")     ## Student's t-test (equal variances, two groups) # When residuals are normally distributed and Levene's test indicates # homoscedasticity, the classic Student's t-test with pooled variance is used df <- droplevels(subset(PlantGrowth, group %in% c("ctrl", "trt1"))) visstat(df$group,df$weight)    ## Welch's t-test (unequal variances, two groups) # When residuals are normally distributed but Levene's test indicates # heteroscedasticity, Welch's t-test is used visstat(mtcars$am, mtcars$mpg)     ## Wilcoxon rank sum test (non-normal, two groups) # When residuals are not normally distributed grades_gender <- data.frame(   Sex = as.factor(c(rep("Girl", 20), rep("Boy", 20))),   Grade = c(     19.3, 18.1, 15.2, 18.3, 7.9, 6.2, 19.4, 20.3, 9.3, 11.3,     18.2, 17.5, 10.2, 20.1, 13.3, 17.2, 15.1, 16.2, 17.3, 16.5,     5.1, 15.3, 17.1, 14.8, 15.4, 14.4, 7.5, 15.5, 6.0, 17.4,     7.3, 14.3, 13.5, 8.0, 19.5, 13.4, 17.9, 17.7, 16.4, 15.6   ) ) visstat(grades_gender$Sex, grades_gender$Grade)    ## Fisher's ANOVA (equal variances, >2 groups) # When residuals are normally distributed and Levene's test indicates # homoscedasticity, classic Fisher's ANOVA with TukeyHSD post-hoc is used. # Different green letters indicate significant differences between groups.  visstat(PlantGrowth$group, PlantGrowth$weight)    ## Welch's one-way ANOVA (unequal variances, >2 groups) set.seed(123) values <- c(rnorm(20, 10, 1),rnorm(20, 15, 5),rnorm(20, 12, 2)) groups <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 20)) visstat(groups, values)    ## Kruskal-Wallis (non-normal, >2 groups) # When residuals are not normally distributed, kruskal.test() is followed by  # pairwise.wilcox.test.  visstat(iris$Species, iris$Petal.Width)    ## Simple linear regression (both numeric) visstat(trees$Height, trees$Girth, conf.level = 0.99)    ## Pearson's Chi-squared test (both factors, large expected counts) HairEyeColorDataFrame <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(HairEyeColor)) visstat(HairEyeColorDataFrame$Eye, HairEyeColorDataFrame$Hair)    ## Fisher's exact test (both factors, small expected counts) HairEyeColorMaleFisher <- HairEyeColor[, , 1] blackBrownHazelGreen <- HairEyeColorMaleFisher[1:2, 3:4] blackBrownHazelGreen <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(blackBrownHazelGreen)) visstat(blackBrownHazelGreen$Eye, blackBrownHazelGreen$Hair)   ## Save PNG visstat(blackBrownHazelGreen$Hair, blackBrownHazelGreen$Eye,         graphicsoutput = "png", plotDirectory = tempdir())  ## Custom plot name visstat(iris$Species, iris$Petal.Width,         graphicsoutput = "pdf", plotName = "kruskal_iris", plotDirectory = tempdir()) #> Warning: calling par(new=TRUE) with no plot

title: Automated Visualization of Statistical Hypothesis Testing — visstat_core
text: decision logic selecting statistical test described . details, please refer package's vignette("visStatistics"). Throughout, data class numeric integer referred numeric, data class factor referred categorical. significance level alpha defined one minus confidence level, given argument conf.level. Assumptions normality homoscedasticity considered met corresponding test yields p-value greater alpha = 1 - conf.level. choice statistical tests performed visstat_core() depends whether data numeric categorical, number levels categorical variable, distribution data, chosen conf.level. function prioritises interpretable visual output tests remain valid assumptions, following logic : (1) response numerical predictor categorical, tests central tendencies performed. decision logic, please refer packages vignette vignette("visStatistics") (2): response predictor numerical, linear model lm() fitted, residual diagnostics confidence band plot. (3): variables categorical, visstat_core() uses chisq.test() fisher.test() depending expected counts, following Cochran's rule (Cochran (1954) <doi:10.2307/3001666>). Implemented main tests: t.test(), wilcox.test(), aov(), oneway.test(), lm(), kruskal.test(), fisher.test(), chisq.test(). Implemented tests assumptions: Normality: shapiro.test() ad.test() Heteroscedasticity: bartlett.test() levene.test() bp_test() Implemented post hoc tests: TukeyHSD() aov() games.howell  oneway.test() pairwise.wilcox.test() kruskal.test()

title: Automated Visualization of Statistical Hypothesis Testing — visstat_core
code: # Welch Two Sample t-test (t.test()) visstat_core(mtcars, "mpg", "am")   #> Warning: Statistical assumptions violated: #> Homoscedasticity violated (Breusch-Pagan p = 0.0242 ) #> Analysis proceeded but interpret results cautiously. #> RECOMMENDATION: Consider exploring alternatives outside visstat() such as data transformations, generalised linear models, or robust regression. For an assumption-free, non-causal alternative consider rerunning with correlation = TRUE.  ## Wilcoxon rank sum test (wilcox.test()) grades_gender <- data.frame(   Sex = as.factor(c(rep("Girl", 20), rep("Boy", 20))),   Grade = c(     19.3, 18.1, 15.2, 18.3, 7.9, 6.2, 19.4,     20.3, 9.3, 11.3, 18.2, 17.5, 10.2, 20.1, 13.3, 17.2, 15.1, 16.2, 17.3,     16.5, 5.1, 15.3, 17.1, 14.8, 15.4, 14.4, 7.5, 15.5, 6.0, 17.4,     7.3, 14.3, 13.5, 8.0, 19.5, 13.4, 17.9, 17.7, 16.4, 15.6   ) ) visstat_core(grades_gender, "Grade", "Sex")    ## Welch's oneway ANOVA not assuming equal variances (oneway.test()) anova_npk <- visstat_core(npk, "yield", "block")   anova_npk # prints summary of tests #> Object of class 'visstat' #>  #> Available components: #> [1] "summary statistics of ANOVA" "post-hoc analysis "          #> [3] "conf.level"                   ## Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test (kruskal.test()) visstat_core(iris, "Petal.Width", "Species")   visstat_core(InsectSprays, "count", "spray")    ## Simple linear regression  (lm()) visstat_core(trees, "Girth", "Height", conf.level = 0.99)    ## Pearson's Chi-squared test (chisq.test()) ### Transform array to data.frame HairEyeColorDataFrame <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(HairEyeColor)) visstat_core(HairEyeColorDataFrame, "Hair", "Eye")    ## Fisher's exact test (fisher.test()) HairEyeColorMaleFisher <- HairEyeColor[, , 1] ### slicing out a 2 x2 contingency table blackBrownHazelGreen <- HairEyeColorMaleFisher[1:2, 3:4] blackBrownHazelGreen <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(blackBrownHazelGreen)) fisher_stats <- visstat_core(blackBrownHazelGreen, "Hair", "Eye")

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-12

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Unified normality testing across branches. two-group case (t-test vs Wilcoxon) multi-group case (ANOVA vs Kruskal-Wallis) now share normality test: shapiro.test() applied internally studentized residuals lm(y ~ x) via rstandard(). Variance homogeneity test changed bartlett.test() levene.test(). Levene-Brown-Forsythe test (using median) robust non-normality Bartlett’s test. test now determines whether Student’s t-test (t.test(var.equal = TRUE)) Welch’s t-test used two-group case, whether aov() oneway.test() used multi-group case. Large-sample threshold changed 30 50 per group. every group exceeds 50 observations, normality testing skipped parametric tests applied directly (justified central limit theorem). Post-hoc test selection. oneway.test() (Welch’s ANOVA) used unequal variances, games.howell() appropriate post-hoc test, assume equal variances. TukeyHSD() remains aov() (Student’s ANOVA). Rank correlation (correlation = TRUE). Selects appropriate rank correlation data type: Spearman’s ρ\rho two numeric variables, Kendall’s τb\tau_b variables ordered factors. test decision made automatically; requires explicit user choice.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Formula interface: visstat(y ~ x, data = df) now supported alongside existing visstat(x, y) visstat(dataframe, "namey", "namex") forms. Ordered factor responses: response class ordered, visstat() converts numeric ranks applies Wilcoxon Kruskal-Wallis. variables ordered correlation = TRUE, Kendall’s τb\tau_b used instead. Correlation analysis: New parameter correlation visstat(). set TRUE, selects Spearman rank correlation numeric variables Kendall’s τb\tau_b two ordered factors, instead fitting regression model. New exported functions: levene.test(): Levene-Brown-Forsythe test homogeneity variance (center = median), mimicking default behaviour leveneTest() car package. bp.test(): Breusch-Pagan test heteroscedasticity linear regression models. games.howell(): Games-Howell post-hoc test pairwise comparisons following Welch’s ANOVA. vis_numeric(): Visualisation numeric-numeric relationships (regression correlation). vis_group_normality(): Diagnostic plots Welch t-test / Welch ANOVA branch. vis_lm_assumptions(): Renamed vis_anova_assumptions(), now provides unified assumption diagnostics general linear model (t-test, ANOVA, regression).

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: vis_anova_assumptions() removed replaced vis_lm_assumptions(). new function handles ANOVA regression diagnostics (controlled regression parameter). regression, shows Residuals vs Leverage plot (Cook’s distance contours) Breusch-Pagan test instead Bartlett test.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: vis_anova_assumptions() provided deprecated wrapper vis_lm_assumptions(). removed future version.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Diagnostic plot title now reads “Linear model assumptions” test p-values two lines. plot.visstat() method added visstat class. Diagnostic plots normality now include histogram overlaid normal density curve. assumption plots saved prefix “assumption”.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Vignette substantially revised: Spearman correlation section rewritten, justification Spearman-approach added. Complete decision logic rewritten reflect new test selection algorithm. general linear model framework (Appendix ) introduced. Clarified rstandard() computes internally studentized residuals, reference Cook Weisberg (1982). Bibliography extended. DESCRIPTION rewritten reflect updated test selection algorithm.
